title: Notched-Stringer Outdoor Chaise Lounge
summary: A reclining outdoor lounge chair with a hinged back that folds flat, built from 1x2 and 1x4 stock with a slatted seat and a simple dowel-and-strap incline catch.
description:
The whole reclining mechanism comes down to one small part: a 3/4" dowel riding in pipe straps at the end of the back's incline supports, positioned so it folds up flush with the last back slat when the chair lies flat. There's no adjustable ratchet or notched bracket here — the dowel simply catches against the seat's last slat at whatever angle you rest the back at, which is why the incline supports need their dowel mounted at the very end, not partway down.

The back-to-seat hinge is deliberately positioned in the gap between the last back slat and the last seat slat rather than centered on either board, so the hinge pin doesn't bind as the back swings through its full range. Three hinges spread across the back stringers is enough for a lounger — this isn't a hinge doing structural work, just a pivot, since the incline support and dowel carry the actual load of a reclined back.

tools.note: Cuts the notches in the side stringers and the leg tapers.

materials.name: Outdoor foam, 24" wide, 48" and 30" lengths
materials.note: Two pieces for the seat and back cushions.

cutList.note: Notched for the base support, inside legs, and back hinge line.
Cut to a taper to match the outer leg.
Tapered to match the inside leg piece.

steps.title: Notch the side stringers
steps.body: Notch out the side stringers as shown in the plan — you can add extra notches beyond the plan's if you want more customization.

steps.title: Attach the base support
steps.body: Fasten the base support between the side stringers with glue and 2" screws.

steps.title: Attach the sides
steps.body: Fasten the sides to the side stringers, keeping the bottom edges flush, with glue and 1-1/4" screws. The front and back ends of the stringers will sit about 4-1/4" proud of the sides.

steps.title: Attach the inside legs
steps.body: Cut the inside leg pieces to a taper, then fasten them to the sides with glue and 1-1/4" screws.

steps.title: Attach the stretchers
steps.body: Fasten the stretchers to the inner legs with 2" screws and glue.

steps.title: Finish the legs
steps.body: Cut the taper on the outer leg pieces to match the inner legs, then fasten outer to inner with 1-1/4" nails through the insides. Fasten the front (or back, on the back legs) pieces to both the inner and outer leg pieces.

steps.title: Attach the main slats
steps.body: Starting with the front 1x4 piece and the last slat that lands on the base support, fasten the main slats to the stringers with 2" screws and glue, spacing them about 1-1/2" apart.

steps.title: Build the back
steps.body: Build the back frame, keeping the top and bottom edges flush with the back stringers — the slats will sit about 1" proud of the stringers. Fasten through the back stringers into the backs of the slats with 1-1/4" screws to keep the screw heads hidden.

steps.title: Attach the back to the lounge
steps.body: Lay the back in the lounger and attach its last slat to the lounger's last slat with three hinges, all positioned over the back stringers. Lay each hinge flat on top of the slats so the pin rests in the gap between the two slats.

steps.title: Add the incline supports
steps.body: Attach the dowel to the incline supports using the pipe straps, positioning it at the very end of the supports so it folds up flush with the top when the lounger lies flat. Hinge the incline supports to the 1x4 back slat.

steps.title: Check the recline
steps.body: Fold the back down flat and confirm the dowel rests just above the last seat slat — this is what lets the back lie flat and still catch at a reclined angle.

steps.title: Finish and cushion
steps.body: Fill screw holes with wood filler, sand every surface, and apply an outdoor-rated finish. Cut the foam to fit the seat and back and cover with fabric to make the two cushions.

images.alt: Outdoor chaise lounge with a hinged, reclining back
